title: From The Whole To A Part
study: A Study In The Life Of John The
scriptureForWeek: VARIOUS PASSAGES IN THE GOSPELS Various passages dealing with John the Baptist's confrontation with Herod, his imprisonment, and his death.
weekTheme: Boys Question; Men Accept God will confront you “We can be in our day what the heroes of faith were in their day - but remember at the time they didn't know they were heroes.” - A.W. Tozer You are growing up in a world where everyone thinks their story is promised a happy ending. We have been discipled by Disney to think that everything will turn out fine if we just believe. But life isn’t a Disney movie. Life is a war. Sometimes the good guys die. Sometimes evil wins the moment. Sometimes things don’t turn out how we want. The most important thing isn’t how long we live; it’s how well we live. It’s whether or not we fulfill the purpose and calling God has for us, not whether we live to be an old man. If you don’t understand the nature of the story you are in, you wont be able to live properly. John the Baptist is now coming to the end of his life. He has played his part, made history, ushered in the ministry of Jesus and lived with conviction. But his time on the stage is coming to a close. Walt Wittman, a famous American poet asks the question of what the purpose of life is about. He asks, The question, O me! so sad, recurring—What good amid these, O me, O life? Answer. That you are here—that life exists and identity, That the powerful play goes on, and you may contribute a verse. The powerful play of redemptive history goes on. John the Baptist contributed his verse, and now you are on the stage contributing yours.

days.title: Dealing With Your Doubts
days.scripture: LUKE 7:18-23 John’s disciples told him about all these things. Calling two of them, he sent them to the Lord to ask, “Are you the one who is to come, or should we expect someone else?” When the men came to Jesus, they said, “John the Baptist sent us to you to ask, ‘Are you the one who is to come, or should we expect someone else?’” At that very time Jesus cured many who had diseases, sicknesses and evil spirits, and gave sight to many who were blind. So he replied to the messengers, “Go back and report to John what you have seen and heard: The blind receive sight, the lame walk, those who have leprosy are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, and the good news is proclaimed to the poor. Blessed is anyone who does not stumble on account of me.”
days.reflection: Have you ever struggled with doubts in your life? Doubts about God? Doubts about faith? Doubts about God’s goodness? If so, you are not alone. The Psalms are filled with people calling out to God, asking for help in their suffering. Our world is filled with people crying out to God too. John the Baptist reminds us that it is natural and normal to struggle with doubts and fears in our faith. Even the strongest and most faithful believers may go through seasons of doubt and questioning, especially in the midst of difficult circumstances. It is important to remember that doubts are not a sign of weakness, but an opportunity for growth and deeper understanding. God can handle your doubts. In Matthew 11:2-3, John sends his disciples to Jesus with the question, "Are you the one who is to come, or should we expect someone else?" This question shows that John was struggling with his faith in Jesus, despite the fact that he had previously baptized him and witnessed the Holy Spirit descending on him. John has been faithful and courageous; yet, now he is in prison. He doesn’t seem to be getting out anytime soon. He knows that Jesus has the power to set him free, but Jesus doesn’t seem to be doing anything about his situation. He was probably wondering why Jesus, whom he had believed to be the Messiah, had not yet intervened on his behalf. In his moment of doubt, John may have been hoping for a different kind of Messiah, one who would overthrow the Roman government and free the Jewish people from oppression, and free him in the process. Despite John's doubts, Jesus does not rebuke him or criticize him for his lack of faith. Instead, Jesus sends a message back to John, telling him about the miracles he has performed and the good news he has preached to the poor. Jesus encourages John to hold on to his faith, even in the midst of difficult circumstances. The story of John's doubt can teach us a valuable lesson about how to keep hope in hard situations where God seems absent. We can take comfort in the fact that even great prophets like John can struggle with doubt and uncertainty. We can also remember that God does not abandon us in our times of need, even when we feel like he is far away. Charles Spurgeon, an amazing 19th-century preacher once said: "God is too good to be unkind and He is too wise to be mistaken. And when we cannot trace His hand, we must trust His heart." It’s hard to trust God's heart when things are hard, but that’s what you are going to have to learn to do. You will go through hard and heartbreaking situations in your life that stretch your faith and stress your heart. Don’t shrink back. Don’t look for another messiah. Play your role and be faithful to the end. It's okay to struggle; it’s not okay to bail. Play your part, and play it well. Trust God. That’s all that God asks of any man, and all he will ask of you.
questions: Have you ever struggled with doubt in God's goodness because of something you have been through? Would you be willing to share what it was, and why it was so hard?
What are some of the things that make guys your age struggle with their faith in God? Do you know how you could respond to them to encourage them?
Ask the person you are doing this study with about a time they wrestled with doubt because of what they were going through. What was it, how did God speak to them, and how did they get through it?

thoughts.verse: And you must show mercy to those whose faith is wavering. Rescue others by snatching them from the flames of judgment.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to be honest with God in any areas you are struggling with doubt in. Offer them to God and invite him into them.

days.title: Seeking Not To Stumble
days.scripture: LUKE 7:18-23 John’s disciples told him about all these things. Calling two of them, he sent them to the Lord to ask, “Are you the one who is to come, or should we expect someone else?” When the men came to Jesus, they said, “John the Baptist sent us to you to ask, ‘Are you the one who is to come, or should we expect someone else?’” At that very time Jesus cured many who had diseases, sicknesses and evil spirits, and gave sight to many who were blind. So he replied to the messengers, “Go back and report to John what you have seen and heard: The blind receive sight, the lame walk, those who have leprosy are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, and the good news is proclaimed to the poor. Blessed is anyone who does not stumble on account of me.”
days.reflection: Charles Swindoll, a Christian pastor and author, once said: "I am convinced that life is 10% what happens to me and 90% of how I react to it." That’s kind of a crazy statement. Is it really even possible to think like that? How can you not freak out, react, and blame other people when bad things happen to you? Yet, it’s a statement that many men have found to be true over the years as they grow up, accept their place in the story of God, and find their strength in him. One of the greatest things you have to learn as you grow into a man is how to choose your response to difficult circumstances. When we face trials and adversity, it can be easy to become discouraged, bitter, or resentful. But if we can cultivate a spirit of faithfulness, we can find strength, hope, and purpose even in the midst of our suffering. John the Baptist has been imprisoned by Herod for speaking the truth to him about his unlawful marriage. He is being held in prison against his own will, with a powerful woman waiting for the right time to kill him. So when he sends a message to Jesus, asking what's going to happen to him, Jesus sends him a fascinating and challenging reply. “Blessed is anyone who does not stumble on account of me.” Jesus is telling John here to stand strong and accept the conditions he is in. He is telling him to change his perspective because he is not going to change his circumstances. John could be angry at Jesus. John could be angry at God. John could regret being so bold and leading with so much conviction. Yet Jesus is telling him to stand strong and not throw his confession and confidence away. He is telling John to be bold and brave and loyal to the end. He is telling him to accept the hard parts of the story as well as the good parts. He is telling him to keep his faith and not throw it away at the last minute just because things are getting hard. You are going to face so many things in the coming years that will make guys your age want to stumble. You will face sexual temptation, conflict with your friends, being mocked or made fun of for being a Christian, and you will be tempted to shrink back, shut up, or shut down. But remember this verse from Hebrews 10:35- 36: So do not throw away your confidence; it will be richly rewarded. You need to persevere so that when you have done the will of God, you will receive what he has promised. Faithfulness in hard times leads to rich rewards. It leads to answers to prayers and promises from God. Though it can be hard to focus on these kinds of things when you are dealing with drama, remember the 10/90 rule. Life is 10% what happens to me and 90% of how I react to it. Ask God for grace to react in light of the rich rewards you will get, not the pain and struggles of the moment. It will absolutely be worth it in the end. Stay strong; don’t stumble.
questions: Life can be really hard, and temptations and struggles can seem overwhelming. What do you think are some of the biggest issues that temp guys your age to give up on their faith?
Do you agree with the statement by Charles Swindoll: ”I am convinced that life is 10% what happens to me and 90% of how I react to it.” Why or why not?
Ask the person you are doing this study with how they learned to choose their reaction to their situations, rather than just reacting to them. Ask them for a specific example.

thoughts.verse: And let us run with endurance the race God has set before us. We do this by keeping our eyes on Jesus, the champion who initiates and perfects our faith. Because of the joy awaiting him, he endured the cross, disregarding its shame. Now he is seated in the place of honor beside God’s throne. Think of all the hostility he endured from sinful people; then you won’t become weary and give up.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a fresh perspective of how you can react in a mature and godly way to anything you are dealing with right now.

days.title: Courage And Faith
days.scripture: LUKE 7:18-23 John’s disciples told him about all these things. Calling two of them, he sent them to the Lord to ask, “Are you the one who is to come, or should we expect someone else?” When the men came to Jesus, they said, “John the Baptist sent us to you to ask, ‘Are you the one who is to come, or should we expect someone else?’” At that very time Jesus cured many who had diseases, sicknesses and evil spirits, and gave sight to many who were blind. So he replied to the messengers, "Go back and report to John what you have seen and heard: The blind receive sight, the lame walk, those who have leprosy are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, and the good news is proclaimed to the poor. Blessed is anyone who does not stumble on account of me.”
days.reflection: One of the reasons we shrink back when we have to do something hard is that we are afraid. It’s hard to do the right thing when you feel torn inside. And these struggles can distort our perspective. We think we must be cowards or lack courage if we have fear and anxiety in our hearts. We can fall into the trap of thinking brave people aren’t afraid and are full of confidence and conviction. But that is rarely true. Most men are just as afraid as you are, but some of them act in spite of these feelings. They don’t feel differently; they just act differently. John Eldredge once wrote, "Courage is not the absence of fear; it is the presence of faith." This quote reminds us that courage is not the same as fearlessness; rather, it is the willingness to act in the face of fear, trusting in God's guidance and provision. This is what we see when John is imprisoned. He is wrestling with the circumstances in his heart, but he doesn’t let those struggles make him compromise his faith. More than likely, he would have been released if he simply backed down, softened his critique, and told Herod and Herodias their marriage was fine. Yet in spite of his questions (are you the one or should we expect someone else?), he stayed loyal and strong. He let his faith overcome his feelings, even to the point of death. If you are going to play your part in the story of God, you are going to have to learn to manage your fear and act in the face of it. Trying to get rid of fear is often a waste of time, but learning to act in spite of it is the way of men. One of the best ways to do this is to evaluate the different kinds of fear you have. If you fear the right thing, it lessens your fear of the wrong thing. If you fear God, you won’t fear what others think as much. If you fear displeasing God, you won’t be as tempted to please others. If you fear missing opportunities, you won’t be tempted to waste your time. That’s why Jesus told us to fear the right things, so we don’t worry about the wrong things. He said. "Do not be afraid of those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul. Rather, be afraid of the One who can destroy both soul and body in hell." Fearing God helped John the Baptist be fear-less. He was able to stay loyal in spite of his struggles. And learning to do this when it counts is really how you measure a man. Martin Luther King Jr said, "The ultimate measure of a man is not where he stands in moments of comfort and convenience, but where he stands at times of challenge and controversy." God will strengthen your heart when things get hard. The fear may not disappear, but he will give you the courage and strength to handle it when you need to. Resolve to stay loyal to your call, no matter the cost. Aren’t you grateful that Jesus did?
questions: Can you think of a moment or two in your life when you acted with courage and overcame your fears? What was it, and how did you do that?
If you got to visit John the Baptist when he was in prison at this stage of his life, what would you say to him?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time they were really afraid but acted in faith anyway. What was it, and what happened as a result? How did you overcome your feelings of fear?

thoughts.verse: I have told you all this so that you may have peace in me. Here on earth you will have many trials and sorrows. But take heart, because I have overcome the world.”
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you his peace in the middle of what is causing you stress and anxiety in your life right now.

days.title: The Closing Act
days.scripture: MATTHEW 14:2-5 When Herod Antipas, the ruler of Galilee, heard about Jesus, he said to his advisers, “This must be John the Baptist raised from the dead! That is why he can do such miracles.” For Herod had arrested and imprisoned John as a favor to his wife Herodias (the former wife of Herod’s brother Philip). John had been telling Herod, “It is against God’s law for you to marry her.” Herod wanted to kill John, but he was afraid of a riot, because all the people believed John was a prophet. But at a birthday party for Herod, Herodias’s daughter performed a dance that greatly pleased him, so he promised with a vow to give her anything she wanted. At her mother’s urging, the girl said, “I want the head of John the Baptist on a tray!” Then the king regretted what he had said; but because of the vow he had made in front of his guests, he issued the necessary orders. So John was beheaded in the prison, and his head was brought on a tray and given to the girl, who took it to her mother. Later, John’s disciples came for his body and buried it. Then they went and told Jesus what had happened.
days.reflection: Memento mori. Have you heard those words before? Do you know what they mean? When you are young, you tend to think that you are going to live forever. Death can seem distant and vague, a long way off. Memento mori is the idea that it is important for us to remember that life is shorter than we think and that death is inevitable. It’s a hard thing to take in, but important if you are going to learn to live well. Remember Psalm 90:12? Teach us to realize the brevity of life, so that we may grow in wisdom. If we know we only have a certain amount of time on the stage of history, we will seek to play our part well. If we know our life is short, we will use our time well. If we know we will give an account of how we spend our time on earth, we will seek to be faithful to God. Men realize the shortness of life; boys never give it a thought. Men seek to be faithful and leave a godly legacy; boys think about having fun in the moment. Men are willing to give their lives for the right thing; boys compromise to avoid trouble. And this brings us back to the tragic death of John the Baptist. It wasn’t fair that John was killed because of a woman’s anger. It wasn’t fair that his head was cut off and carried on a tray by a teenage girl who was forced into a provocative dance to please older men. But life isn’t fair, and the forces of evil that we are up against are real and potent. But even though John the Baptist's story came to a tragic end, he played his part well. He prepared his generation for the coming of Jesus. He turned people’s hearts back to God. He lived with conviction and integrity in front of a watching world. And he died remaining loyal to his calling. It’s important to remember that our life is not measured by the length of our days but by the quality of our days. It's not measured by how much fun we can have but by how fruitful we are. It’s not measured by worldly standards of success, but by God's standards of faithfulness. One day, you will die (hopefully in a more gracious way than JTB). Why not take a few minutes today to make sure that you do it with your head held high, leaving a legacy of integrity, courage, and deep conviction?
questions: Why do you think it’s important to have an awareness of how short your life will be as a guy your age?
Do you think it’s better to live a long life of pleasure or a short life of meaning and significance? Why?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share how an awareness of time and the shortness of life has affected their choices. Ask them to be specific.

thoughts.verse: How do you know what your life will be like tomorrow? Your life is like the morning fog—it’s here a little while, then it’s gone.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you from it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a vision of being faithful through all the stages and seasons of your life.

days.title: Leaving The Stage
days.scripture: MATTHEW 14:1-12 When Herod Antipas, the ruler of Galilee, heard about Jesus, he said to his advisers, “This must be John the Baptist raised from the dead! That is why he can do such miracles.” For Herod had arrested and imprisoned John as a favor to his wife Herodias (the former wife of Herod’s brother Philip). John had been telling Herod, “It is against God’s law for you to marry her.” Herod wanted to kill John, but he was afraid of a riot, because all the people believed John was a prophet. But at a birthday party for Herod, Herodias’s daughter performed a dance that greatly pleased him, so he promised with a vow to give her anything she wanted. At her mother’s urging, the girl said, “I want the head of John the Baptist on a tray!” Then the king regretted what he had said; but because of the vow he had made in front of his guests, he issued the necessary orders. So John was beheaded in the prison, and his head was brought on a tray and given to the girl, who took it to her mother. Later, John’s disciples came for his body and buried it. Then they went and told Jesus what had happened.
days.reflection: All the world’s a stage, And all the men and women merely players; They have their exits and their entrances; And one man in his time plays many parts. Those are some of William Shakespeare’s most famous lines. They have endured throughout time because they speak about something that every person experiences: birth and death. We enter the stage of history, we live, and we leave the stage of history. Life is such an amazing gift from God. But each person plays a different role on the stage, and not all people play their part equally well. For the last nine weeks, you have been studying the life of John the Baptist. You have been learning from him how to make a massive shift from being a boy into becoming a man; the shift from thinking the whole story is about you, to being content to play your part. And John the Baptist’s life teaches us that so well. He bursts onto the scene, stirs up the whole nation, points people to Jesus, and then his time is up. The last thing we read about his life is this: John’s disciples came for his body and buried it. Then they went and told Jesus what had happened. John is buried, Jesus is informed, and now the story continues. John has played his part, the baton is passed to Jesus, and now the main character can play his role. From this point on, the gospel will talk about Jesus. John did decrease, and Jesus increased, and John would be absolutely thrilled with that. Yet Jesus’ ministry would not have happened like it did if John the Baptist did not set the stage. He prepared the hearts of the people, shook the culture out of its apathy, gave them a hunger for something better, and joyfully gave the influence and attention to Jesus when he showed up. God has given you a set amount of time on this earth to serve him. There was a day appointed for your birth, and there is a day coming when you die. What you are learning right now is an important part of figuring out what your role is and how to play it well. So many guys wander through life not really knowing why they were born and numbing their frustration with stuff that doesn’t help them grow up and doesn’t heal their pain. You have a real gift and a real head start working on this stuff now. You may not see how it all fits together, but as you keep moving ahead, you will begin to see the difference between those who play their role well and those who squander it. You were put here for a purpose. You were put here to point to Jesus. Keep moving forward and you will play your role well.
questions: Ask the person you are doing this study with to share what they would redo if they had the chance to live their teenage years over again.
What were some of the things they did in those years that helped them play their role well, and what is some advice they could give you now?
Do you ever think about the day of your death? What are some of the things you hope to do before you die?

thoughts.verse: But each one should build with care. For no one can lay any foundation other than the one already laid, which is Jesus Christ. If anyone builds on this foundation using gold, silver, costly stones, wood, hay or straw, their work will be shown for what it is, because the Day will bring it to light. It will be revealed with fire, and the fire will test the quality of each person’s work. If what has been built survives, the builder will receive a reward. If it is burned up, the builder will suffer loss but yet will be saved—even though only as one escaping through the flames.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to fill your heart with a desire to please him, and to know that when you stand before him you will hear “well done good and faithful servant.”

extras: WEEK 9 MANSCHOOL EULOGY MANSCHOOL REMINDER The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner. - John Eldredge OPTION 1. ACTIVITY: EULOGY “All men die, not all men truly live,” said William Wallace. Most guys this age never really give a thought about the legacy they will leave behind or the kinds of things they want to be said about them when they are gone. Explain what a funeral eulogy is and why it’s so powerful, and then have them take time to think about what they want to be said about them when they are gone. You may want to watch a few YouTube examples so eulogies so they get the idea or share a couple you may have given in your life. OPTION 2. WATCH Lincoln An Oscar-worthy performance from Daniel Day-Lewis that tells the story of Lincoln's vision to outlaw slavery and establish it in the constitution. A touch historical, but a deeply moving account of a man who prepared a way for freedom but never got to see the vision in full. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KJVuqYkI2jQ OPTION 3. DINNER AND DISCUSSION Take your son out to one of his favorite restaurants or for desserts and talk through what it means to struggle with not being the center of attention. There is a fine balance between being ignored and voluntarily giving attention away out of a secure sense of self.
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
